_description: Known issues, root causes, and solutions — maintained by QA-Agent and all agents

patterns.title: Next.js version mismatch in monorepo
patterns.symptoms: Build fails with 'Jest worker encountered child process exceptions' or Turbopack internal error
patterns.root_cause: Root monorepo has next@16.1.1, mission-control needs 15.5.4. npx resolves to root version.
patterns.solution: Use node_modules/.bin/next build instead of npx next build
patterns.recurrence: Every time someone runs npx next

patterns.title: SSR hydration mismatch with dynamic values
patterns.symptoms: React hydration error: server rendered text didn't match client
patterns.root_cause: Using Date.now(), Math.random(), or locale-dependent formatting in SSR components
patterns.solution: Move dynamic values to useEffect or pass stable values from server
patterns.recurrence: When adding date displays or random IDs in page components

patterns.title: Agent Hub connection timeout
patterns.symptoms: Chat returns OpenAI fallback, API routes return cached: true
patterns.root_cause: Agent Hub server on localhost:3000 not running or network timeout (3s limit)
patterns.solution: Start Agent Hub server. Check AGENT_HUB_LIVE env var. Cached data serves as fallback.
patterns.recurrence: After system restart or when Agent Hub is updating

patterns.title: Agent Hub execute returns empty on long analytical prompts
patterns.symptoms: MCP execute call returns empty content for prompts requesting structured analysis (8+ lesson extraction)
patterns.root_cause: Likely token limit or timeout on stateless /execute endpoint. Agent starts tool calls but response exceeds output token limit (4000-6000 tokens).
patterns.solution: Break large prompts into smaller focused queries. Or use session-based chat for long interactions. Orchestrator hello works (short response), analysis fails (long response).
patterns.recurrence: When using MCP execute for complex analytical tasks

patterns.title: pnpm hard-link corruption on Windows 11
patterns.symptoms: node_modules files show as directories with year 1601 timestamps. npm install fails.
patterns.root_cause: pnpm uses hard-links from global store. Windows NTFS sometimes corrupts these links during forced shutdown or antivirus scan.
patterns.solution: PowerShell Remove-Item -Recurse -Force on node_modules, then npm install --no-workspaces instead of pnpm.
patterns.recurrence: On Windows systems using pnpm with hard-links

patterns.title: GET /api/system/config has no authentication — publicly accessible endpoint
patterns.symptoms: Any unauthenticated HTTP client can call GET /api/system/config and receive system metadata. The route.ts has zero auth checks, no session validation, no middleware guard.
patterns.root_cause: The route handler calls readUIConfig() directly without verifying the caller's session. The comment in the file claims rate limiting is enforced via Next.js middleware, but no middleware file was found protecting /api/system/* routes.
patterns.solution: Add session check at the top of the GET handler: `const session = await getServerSession(); if (!session) return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 });`. Alternatively, add /api/system/* to the auth middleware matcher. Verify middleware.ts actually covers this path.

patterns.title: TimestampFooter fetch has no timeout — hangs indefinitely on slow network
patterns.symptoms: The useEffect fetch to /api/system/config has no AbortController / timeout. On a slow or stalled connection the footer never renders (isLoaded stays false), and the request is never cancelled when the component unmounts, causing a state-update-on-unmounted-component warning.
patterns.root_cause: fetch() is called without an AbortSignal. No cleanup function is returned from useEffect, so the promise resolves after unmount and calls setLastUpdated / setIsLoaded on a dead component.
patterns.solution: Add `const controller = new AbortController(); const timeoutId = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), 5000);` and pass `{ signal: controller.signal }` to fetch. Return `() => { clearTimeout(timeoutId); controller.abort(); }` from useEffect.

patterns.title: cached:true flag semantics are inverted and misleading — causes silent data staleness
patterns.symptoms: The API response field `cached: false` means "fresh data" and `cached: true` means "error fallback / stale". This is the opposite of every HTTP caching convention. Consumers (current or future) will misread the flag and treat error-fallback data as authoritative cached data.
patterns.root_cause: The field was named `cached` but its value encodes an error state, not a cache hit. The JSDoc comment even admits this: "cached: true → FS read error or JSON parse error (fallback)".
patterns.solution: Rename the field to `error: boolean` or `fallback: boolean`, or add a separate `error` field. Update the interface, the JSDoc, and any consumers. The TimestampFooter currently ignores this field entirely, so it cannot distinguish stale-fallback from fresh data.

patterns.title: ADR-002 violation: getUiConfig() silently swallows ENOENT instead of returning null cleanly
patterns.symptoms: getUiConfig() calls ensureDataDir() before every read, creating the directory on every GET request even when the file doesn't exist. The catch block returns null correctly, but the directory-creation side-effect on every read is unexpected and masks the ENOENT condition instead of propagating it as a clean null per ADR-002.
patterns.root_cause: ensureDataDir() is called inside getUiConfig() unconditionally. ADR-002 says ENOENT should return null, not trigger auto-creation. The directory mkdir is a write side-effect inside a read function, violating command-query separation.
patterns.solution: Remove ensureDataDir() call from getUiConfig(). Only call it inside setUiConfig(). In getUiConfig(), catch ENOENT specifically (err.code === 'ENOENT') and return {lastUpdated: null}; re-throw other errors so the route's catch block can log them properly.

patterns.title: POST /api/system/config has no authentication — any unauthenticated caller can overwrite lastUpdated
patterns.symptoms: POST /api/system/config accepts any body with a valid ISO string and writes it to disk. No session check, no API key, no internal-only guard. Any external actor can set lastUpdated to an arbitrary date (e.g. year 9999) causing misleading UI across all shell pages.
patterns.root_cause: Route handler has no auth middleware or token verification. The comment says "fire-and-forget from pipeline-executor" but the route is publicly reachable.
patterns.solution: Add an internal secret check: compare Authorization header against process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET. Return 401 if missing/wrong. Alternatively, move the write to a server-action callable only from pipeline-executor server-side code, removing the HTTP surface entirely.

patterns.title: setUiConfig atomic write leaves orphaned .tmp file on rename failure
patterns.symptoms: If fs.rename() throws (e.g. cross-device link, permissions), the .tmp file is left on disk permanently. Subsequent calls to setUiConfig will overwrite it, but a crash between writeFile and rename leaves DATA_CONFIG_PATH.tmp polluting the data/config/ directory indefinitely.
patterns.root_cause: No finally block cleans up the tmp file on rename failure. The error propagates to the caller but the orphaned file remains.
patterns.solution: Wrap the rename in try/catch; in the catch, attempt fs.unlink(tmpPath) before re-throwing. Use a unique tmp name (e.g. append process.pid + Date.now()) to avoid collisions under concurrent writes.

patterns.title: Missing rate limiting implementation — documented but not enforced on /api/system/config
patterns.symptoms: Route comments claim "Rate limit: 60 req/min per IP" (GET) and "Rate limit: 10 req/min per IP" (POST) but no rate-limiting middleware, header checks, or Upstash/Redis integration exists in the handler code
patterns.root_cause: Rate limit is documented as a comment only. No actual implementation (e.g., upstash/ratelimit, next-rate-limit, or custom Redis counter) is present in the route file or called from a middleware wrapper.
patterns.solution: Implement rate limiting using @upstash/ratelimit or a sliding-window Redis counter. Apply as a wrapper or in middleware.ts. Enforce 429 response with Retry-After header when limit exceeded.

patterns.title: data/config/ui.json отсутствует в репозитории — первый запуск вернёт null без инициализации
patterns.symptoms: Директория data/config не существует (ENOENT при list_files). При первом запуске getUiConfig() выбросит ошибку чтения файла, GET вернёт { lastUpdated: null, error: "config_unavailable" }, футер не отобразится никогда до первого pipeline-запуска.
patterns.root_cause: Файл data/config/ui.json не создан как часть задачи (задача требовала его создать). Нет seed-скрипта или инициализации при старте приложения.
patterns.solution: 1) Создать data/config/ui.json с начальным значением { "lastUpdated": "<build-time ISO>" }. 2) Добавить файл в .gitignore исключения (не игнорировать). 3) В getUiConfig() добавить fallback: если файл не существует — вернуть { lastUpdated: null } без throw.

patterns.title: TimestampFooter returns null during loading — permanent blank state if API never resolves
patterns.symptoms: Footer never renders if SWR fetch hangs or API returns { lastUpdated: null }. Component returns null for both isLoading AND formattedDate === null, so a successful API response with null lastUpdated also produces no footer — silently swallowing the "no data" state with no user feedback.
patterns.root_cause: Line 45 of timestamp-footer.tsx: `if (isLoading || formattedDate === null) return null` — the null guard conflates "still loading" with "loaded but no timestamp". When config file is missing and API returns { lastUpdated: null }, formattedDate stays null and the footer is permanently invisible.
patterns.solution: Separate loading and empty states. When !isLoading and data is received but lastUpdated is null, render a fallback string (e.g. "Дата обновления недоступна") or at minimum a non-null placeholder. Only suppress render during isLoading.

patterns.title: GET /api/system/config accesses config.lastUpdated without null-guard when getUiConfig returns null per ADR-002
patterns.symptoms: Route.ts line 33: `{ lastUpdated: config.lastUpdated }` — if getUiConfig() returns null (per ADR-002 ENOENT behavior), this throws a TypeError: Cannot read properties of null, which is caught by the outer catch and returns config_unavailable — masking the expected null response as an error.
patterns.root_cause: ADR-002 specifies getUiConfig() should return null on ENOENT, but the GET handler treats the return value as always being an object with a lastUpdated property. No null-check before property access on line 33.
patterns.solution: Add null guard: `const lastUpdated = config ? config.lastUpdated : null; return NextResponse.json({ lastUpdated })`. This correctly returns { lastUpdated: null } (200 OK) when file is missing, matching ADR-002 contract.

patterns.title: Escalation trigger bypass via metadata injection in stepResult
patterns.symptoms: Malicious or malformed agent output can inject arbitrary metadata fields (severity, hasCriticalBug, qualityScore) into stepResult.metadata, triggering or suppressing escalation triggers T1-T5 without legitimate security/QA findings.
patterns.root_cause: checkEscalationTriggers() trusts stepResult.metadata values without validation or schema enforcement. Any agent response that populates metadata fields can control escalation flow. The parseEscalateTag regex is also injectable via crafted agent output.
patterns.solution: 1) Validate stepResult.metadata against a strict schema (zod) before trigger evaluation. 2) Only allow specific agentIds to set specific metadata fields (cyber-agent → severity/probability, qa-agent → hasCriticalBug/qualityScore). 3) Add HMAC or signing to agent outputs to prevent tampering between step execution and trigger check. 4) Sanitize parseEscalateTag input — limit output length before regex parsing.

patterns.title: Zustand persist stores PII (escalation data) unencrypted in localStorage
patterns.symptoms: EscalationReason[], confirmedBy (MEDIUM PII), security finding severities stored in plaintext localStorage via Zustand persist middleware. Accessible via browser DevTools.
patterns.root_cause: orchestration-store.ts uses Zustand persist without partialize filter or encrypted storage adapter. New ADR-012 fields (escalationReasons, escalatedFrom) automatically persisted with full detail strings.
patterns.solution: 1) Use partialize in persist config to exclude escalationReasons.detail and confirmedBy from localStorage. 2) Or implement encrypted storage adapter (AES-256 with session-derived key). 3) Strip sensitive fields in addToHistory before persisting to executionHistory array. This is a 152-FZ Art.19 requirement.

patterns.title: Stateful /g regex in ESCALATION_CONFIG causes intermittent security trigger misses
patterns.symptoms: detectEscalation() fails to detect Critical/High security findings approximately 50% of the time; escalation triggers fire inconsistently across consecutive pipeline steps.
patterns.root_cause: ESCALATION_CONFIG stores regex patterns with the /g flag. RegExp.test() on a global regex advances lastIndex; on the next call with the same regex instance, matching starts from the previous lastIndex position, often returning false.
patterns.solution: Either store patterns as plain strings and create new RegExp() inside detectEscalation() on each invocation, or remove the /g flag from securityCriticalPattern and securityHighPattern since .test() only needs a boolean match.

patterns.title: ADR-008 detectEscalation: stateful /g regex, output injection, XSS in badge, Jira comment injection
patterns.symptoms: 1) Stateful /g regex in ESCALATION_CONFIG causes .test() to miss matches ~50% of calls. 2) Free-text stepResult.output parsed by regex allows any agent to inject trigger keywords forcing false escalation. 3) EscalationBadge renders trigger.reason (agent-controlled string) without sanitization — XSS risk. 4) Jira comment includes unsanitized reason field — ADF injection. 5) No auth on Zustand setEscalation action.
patterns.root_cause: Security detection relies on regex over unstructured text instead of typed severity fields. UI renders agent-controlled strings directly. Jira comment template interpolates untrusted data into ADF. Global regex flag preserves lastIndex across calls.
patterns.solution: 1) Remove /g flag or instantiate new RegExp per call. 2) Use structured stepResult.severity field, not regex on output. 3) Sanitize trigger.reason before DOM rendering (escape HTML entities). 4) Sanitize reason before Jira ADF interpolation. 5) Validate executionId ownership in setEscalation.

patterns.title: POST /api/system/config — no authentication, open write endpoint
patterns.symptoms: POST /api/system/config accepts writes from any anonymous client. No token, no secret, no session check. Returns {"ok":true} for unauthenticated requests.
patterns.root_cause: Route handler has zero authentication middleware. The endpoint is documented as "fire-and-forget from pipeline-executor" but no internal secret or auth guard was implemented. Rate limit exists only as a JSDoc comment with no actual code.
patterns.solution: Add x-internal-secret header check: const secret = req.headers.get("x-internal-secret"); if (secret !== process.env.INTERNAL_API_SECRET) return 401. Generate INTERNAL_API_SECRET at deploy time (32 bytes). pipeline-executor.ts must pass this header. Also validate lastUpdated strictly with ISO 8601 regex before passing to setUiConfig().

patterns.title: CSV Export без санитизации — XSS через CSV Injection в pipeline-analytics.json
patterns.symptoms: Экспортируемый CSV содержит поля agentName/stepId из pipeline-analytics.json без экранирования. Значения вида =CMD|' /C calc'!A0 или @SUM(1+1) выполняются в Excel/LibreOffice при открытии файла.
patterns.root_cause: Архитектура экспорта предполагает прямую сериализацию объектов PipelineExecution и agentStats в CSV без префиксного экранирования специальных символов (=, +, -, @, TAB, CR).
patterns.solution: Перед записью в CSV каждое строковое поле проверять: если начинается с = + - @ TAB CR — добавлять префикс апострофа или оборачивать в кавычки с экранированием. Использовать библиотеку papaparse или ручную sanitize-функцию.

patterns.title: No auth middleware on internal API routes — all agent-hub proxy routes are publicly accessible
patterns.symptoms: All routes under /api/agent-hub/ (sessions, agents, last-activity) have zero authentication checks. Any external caller who discovers the URL can read agent session data, activity timestamps, and agent metadata.
patterns.root_cause: The codebase has no auth middleware layer (no NextAuth, no session cookies, no internal API key validation). The existing sessions/route.ts pattern that the new endpoint copies from also lacks auth. This is a systemic gap, not a single-endpoint issue.
patterns.solution: Create a shared middleware (e.g., lib/middleware/require-auth.ts) that validates session cookies or an internal token header. Apply it to all /api/agent-hub/* routes. Until auth exists, add Next.js middleware.ts matcher to restrict /api/agent-hub/* to same-origin requests via Origin/Referer header check as a stopgap.

patterns.title: All /api/agent-hub/* routes lack authentication — any HTTP client can read agent session/activity data
patterns.symptoms: No auth check in sessions/route.ts or proposed last-activity/route.ts. No middleware.ts protecting /api/ routes. agentHubFetch uses server-side API key to Agent Hub but the Next.js API route itself is publicly accessible.
patterns.root_cause: Project has no auth system implemented yet. API routes were created as internal proxies but are exposed on the public Next.js server without any request validation. The agentIds query param is concatenated into URLs without UUID format validation, enabling potential SSRF via agentHubFetch URL construction.
patterns.solution: 1) Create middleware.ts with matcher for /api/agent-hub/:path* that checks for a session cookie (when auth exists) or at minimum validates X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest header + Origin header matching NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL. 2) In last-activity/route.ts, validate agentIds with regex /^[0-9a-f-]{36}(,[0-9a-f-]{36})*$/ before passing to any fetch. 3) Add structured logging (pino or project logger) with fields: { endpoint, agentIds, ip, userAgent, timestamp } to stdout for 152-FZ Art.19 audit trail. 4) Classify lastActivity as MEDIUM PII in 152fz-checklist.md and ensure no Supabase caching.

patterns.title: All /api/agent-hub/* routes lack authentication — public data exposure
patterns.symptoms: Any unauthenticated HTTP client can call GET /api/agent-hub/sessions, /agents/last-activity etc. and receive agent session data. No middleware, no cookie check, no token validation exists on these routes.
patterns.root_cause: sessions/route.ts (line 5) and all proposed agent-hub API routes have zero auth checks. agentHubFetch authenticates MC→AgentHub via PAT, but nothing authenticates browser→MC.
patterns.solution: Create middleware.ts with matcher /api/agent-hub/:path* that validates session cookie or, as stopgap, checks Origin + X-Requested-With headers. Must be replaced with real session auth before any public deployment. BLOCKS DEPLOY.

patterns.title: Unsanitized JSON key from user input in file-based storage (agent-last-login)
patterns.symptoms: POST /api/agent-last-login accepts arbitrary agentId string and uses it as a key in a JSON file without validation, enabling prototype pollution (__proto__) or storage abuse.
patterns.root_cause: No input validation or allowlist check on agentId before merging into JSON object and writing to disk.
patterns.solution: Validate agentId with strict regex /^[a-z0-9-]{1,64}$/ and reject requests that don't match. Also use Object.create(null) when parsing JSON to prevent prototype pollution.

patterns.title: Path traversal and race condition in agent-last-login file-based storage
patterns.symptoms: POST /api/agent-last-login accepts arbitrary agentId string used as JSON key with no validation. Concurrent fire-and-forget writes from chat route cause read-modify-write race condition losing timestamps. Self-fetch to localhost:3000 creates SSRF vector.
patterns.root_cause: 1) No agentId validation — attacker can inject __proto__, constructor, or oversized keys into JSON via crafted agentId, causing prototype pollution or DoS. 2) Concurrent fs read-modify-write without file locking loses data. 3) Fire-and-forget uses HTTP self-fetch instead of direct function call, exposing internal endpoint.
patterns.solution: 1) In POST handler of app/api/agent-last-login/route.ts: validate agentId with regex /^[a-z0-9-]{1,64}$/ AND whitelist against cachedAgents.map(a=>a.id). 2) Use proper-lockfile or a mutex around read-modify-write cycle. 3) In app/api/chat/route.ts: replace self-fetch with direct function import (e.g., import {updateLastLogin} from '@/lib/agent-last-login' and call updateLastLogin(agentId).catch(...)). Reject __proto__ and constructor keys explicitly.

patterns.title: File-based storage TOCTOU + input validation gaps in agent-last-login
patterns.symptoms: Concurrent POST /api/agent-last-login requests lose timestamps; unsanitized agentId allows prototype pollution via __proto__ key; self-fetch to localhost:3000 breaks in production and exposes internal endpoints.
patterns.root_cause: PAT-002 file-based storage pattern lacks concurrency control. No input validation on agentId before writing to JSON. Fire-and-forget uses HTTP self-fetch instead of direct module import.
patterns.solution: 1) Add mutex/lock around read-modify-write in POST handler. 2) Validate agentId with /^[a-z0-9-]{1,64}$/ regex. 3) Extract file I/O into lib/agent-last-login.ts shared module, import directly in chat/route.ts instead of self-fetch.

patterns.title: Regex-based escalation detection on LLM output is bypassable and injectable
patterns.symptoms: EscalationDetector T1-T5 triggers use regex on raw LLM agent output strings. Malicious or malformed agent output can either suppress escalation (bypass) or force false escalation (injection).
patterns.root_cause: T1/T2 use regex like /\"severity\"\s*:\s*\"critical\"/i on unstructured StepResult.output. LLM output is not validated against a schema before regex matching. An adversarial prompt or hallucinated output could embed "severity":"low" while containing critical findings, or inject [ESCALATE] tags (T5) to force unnecessary escalation and waste tokens.
patterns.solution: Parse agent output into a typed object via zod schema validation BEFORE running trigger detection. Never regex raw LLM strings for security-critical decisions. Add output schema enforcement in StepResult so triggers operate on structured, validated data.

patterns.title: Escalation detector uses regex on untrusted agent output — injection + XSS + privilege escalation risks
patterns.symptoms: ADR-008 escalation-detector.ts parses raw agent output with regex to detect triggers (T1-T5). Agent output is untrusted LLM text rendered in UI badges and Jira comments. No sanitization, no auth on escalation confirmation, confirmedBy field is MEDIUM PII stored in localStorage via zustand persist.
patterns.root_cause: 1) Regex-based detection on LLM output is trivially spoofable — any agent can emit "severity":"critical" to force T1 escalation. 2) EscalationRecord.triggeredBy.reason is rendered in UI badge and Jira comment without sanitization — XSS vector. 3) No authorization check on who confirms/rejects escalation — any browser session can call approveCheckpoint. 4) confirmedBy (MEDIUM PII per 152-FZ) persisted in localStorage via zustand persist — unencrypted. 5) buildEscalationComment uses string comparison triggerId <= "T2" which is lexicographic, not numeric — fragile but not exploitable.
patterns.solution: 1) Validate agent output against structured JSON schema, not regex on raw text. 2) Sanitize reason field with DOMPurify before rendering in React and escape for Jira markdown. 3) Add user identity verification before escalation confirm/reject actions. 4) Encrypt or exclude confirmedBy from localStorage persistence. 5) Use explicit set comparison for trigger priority instead of string comparison.

patterns.title: EscalationRecord.confirmedBy PII stored unencrypted in localStorage via zustand persist
patterns.symptoms: MEDIUM PII field (confirmedBy: user identifier) persisted to browser localStorage in plaintext via zustand persist middleware in orchestration-store.ts. executionHistory[] contains full EscalationRecord including confirmedBy. Any XSS or browser extension can read all user identifiers from localStorage.
patterns.root_cause: orchestration-store.ts uses zustand persist (line 4, 25) which serializes entire state including executionHistory[] to localStorage. EscalationRecord.confirmedBy (workflow.ts:105) is classified MEDIUM PII per 152-FZ but no encryption or redaction is applied before persistence.
patterns.solution: 1. Add a custom serialize function to zustand persist config that redacts or encrypts confirmedBy before writing to localStorage. 2. Alternatively, store confirmedBy only server-side (Timeweb VPS) and keep only a non-PII reference in client state. 3. For 152-FZ Art.19 compliance, MEDIUM PII must be AES-256 encrypted at rest — localStorage is not encrypted at rest.

patterns.title: Escalation detector parses unstructured LLM output — prompt injection can force mode escalation
patterns.symptoms: detectEscalation() uses string matching on agent output to detect severity=critical, qualityScore, filesAffected. Crafted task input containing these keywords triggers false escalation to Full mode.
patterns.root_cause: Trigger detection relies on parsing free-text output strings instead of structured, typed metadata fields from stepResults. No validation that the output actually comes from the expected agent.
patterns.solution: 1. Use only structured stepResults metadata (typed severity enum, numeric qualityScore) for trigger detection — never parse raw output strings. 2. Validate that trigger source agent matches expected agent ID (T1/T2→cyber-agent, T3/T4→qa-agent, T5→architect-agent). 3. Sanitize reason field before Jira sync to prevent PII leakage to external SaaS.

patterns.title: ADR-008 Jira sync route missing auth + action whitelist allows SSRF to Jira API
patterns.symptoms: POST /api/jira/sync has no authentication check. Any client can call it with arbitrary action/body, including the new "mode-escalation" action. The route also casts body.execution directly to PipelineExecution without validation.
patterns.root_cause: app/api/jira/sync/route.ts has no auth middleware, no origin check, no CSRF token. The new "mode-escalation" action will inherit this gap, allowing unauthenticated comment injection into Jira Epics.
patterns.solution: Add same-origin validation (check Origin/Referer header or use internal API token from env). Validate action against explicit whitelist. Validate body schema with zod before passing to jiraSync functions. For "mode-escalation": validate triggerId is T1-T5 enum, fromMode/toMode are valid ExecutionMode values.

patterns.title: Prototype pollution via unsanitized agentId as object key
patterns.symptoms: Crafted agentId values like __proto__, constructor, or toString used as keys in JSON map can pollute Object prototype when assigned via bracket notation map[agentId] = value
patterns.root_cause: recordAgentActivity(agentId) accepts arbitrary string from request body and uses it directly as object key without validation
patterns.solution: Validate agentId with regex /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ and check against known agent list before writing. Apply this pattern to all file-based storage modules that use user-supplied keys.

patterns.title: Path traversal via unsanitized agentId in file-based JSON storage
patterns.symptoms: Attacker sends crafted agentId like "../../etc/passwd" or "__proto__" as JSON key, potentially causing prototype pollution or writing arbitrary keys into the activity file
patterns.root_cause: recordAgentActivity(agentId) accepts raw string from request body and uses it directly as a JSON object key without validation. The agentId flows from POST /api/chat body → recordAgentActivity → map[agentId] = timestamp. No allowlist or format check exists.
patterns.solution: Validate agentId against a strict regex (e.g. /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$/) or check against known agent list before using as object key. Also reject __proto__, constructor, prototype keys to prevent prototype pollution.

patterns.title: X-Forwarded-For trusted without validation in audit log IP extraction
patterns.symptoms: Audit log stores arbitrary attacker-controlled IP addresses; forensic trail is unreliable; 152-FZ compliance audit finds tampered PII (IP is personal data under 152-FZ Art.3)
patterns.root_cause: extractClientIP() splits X-Forwarded-For and takes leftmost value without validating it is a valid IP or that the proxy chain is trusted. Any client can prepend a fake IP.
patterns.solution: 1) Validate extracted IP with net.isIP() before storing. 2) Configure explicit trusted proxy count (e.g., 1 for Vercel) and read from right side of X-Forwarded-For chain minus trusted hops. 3) Store raw header alongside parsed IP for forensic comparison.

patterns.title: audit_log append-only design blocks 152-FZ Art.14 deletion compliance
patterns.symptoms: User requests data deletion under 152-FZ Art.14. app_user role has REVOKE DELETE. Archival cron sets archived=true but never hard-deletes. PII (ip_address, user_id) persists indefinitely.
patterns.root_cause: Schema designed for tamper-proof audit trail (append-only) without a separate superuser deletion pipeline for GDPR/152-FZ compliance. No hard-DELETE cron or admin endpoint exists.
patterns.solution: Add a superuser-owned pg_cron job that hard-DELETEs rows WHERE archived = true AND occurred_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '3 years'. Create a separate superuser-only function for on-demand deletion by user_id to handle Art.14 requests. Document this in the data processing policy.

patterns.title: audit_log lacks 152-FZ deletion pipeline and has IP spoofing + tenant isolation risks
patterns.symptoms: 1) No DELETE path for user PII in append-only audit_log — 152-FZ Art.21 violation. 2) X-Forwarded-For leftmost IP trusted without proxy validation — audit trail poisoning. 3) tenant_id from JWT claim used directly in SET LOCAL without server-side verification — cross-tenant data leak possible.
patterns.root_cause: ADR-016 focused on performance (BIGSERIAL, lateral JOIN) but omitted: (a) PII deletion mechanism beyond archival, (b) IP validation/sanitization in extractClientIP middleware, (c) server-side tenant_id verification before setting RLS context variable.
patterns.solution: 1) Create SECURITY DEFINER function with dedicated data_deletion_officer role for hard-delete of user PII. 2) Read rightmost X-Forwarded-For hop (proxy-set) instead of leftmost (client-set), validate as IP. 3) Resolve tenant_id from users table via jwt.sub, never trust JWT tenant_id claim directly.

patterns.title: Admin PII endpoint missing rate-limit, cache headers, and log sanitization
patterns.symptoms: GET /api/v1/admin/users exposes admin emails, names, and activity timestamps without rate limiting — enabling account enumeration and active-admin fingerprinting. Fire-and-forget log writes adminId to file-based data/logs.json which may not reside on Timeweb VPS. No Cache-Control headers prevent browser/CDN caching of PII response.
patterns.root_cause: No rate-limit middleware exists in app/api/. Log storage (lib/logs-storage.ts) writes to local filesystem data/logs.json — location depends on deployment host, not guaranteed Timeweb. API contract specifies 30 req/min but no enforcement mechanism exists in codebase. No response header policy for admin routes.
patterns.solution: 1) Add rate-limit middleware in app/api/admin/users/route.ts using Map-based sliding window (no new deps) — 30 req/min keyed by JWT sub claim. 2) In same route file, set headers: Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache; Pragma: no-cache; X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. 3) In fire-and-forget log call, replace raw adminId with HMAC-SHA256(adminId, process.env.LOG_HMAC_SECRET) using Node crypto.createHmac — preserves audit correlation without storing reversible PII. 4) Verify data/logs.json path resolves to Timeweb VPS in deployment config.

patterns.title: PII leakage via file-based logs — adminId written to data/logs.json outside Timeweb PG
patterns.symptoms: Fire-and-forget log spec writes adminId (PII-adjacent UUID linked to a physical person) into local file data/logs.json via addLog(). This file resides on the Next.js deployment host, NOT Timeweb PostgreSQL. If deployed to Vercel/non-Russian hosting, this violates 152-FZ Art.18 data residency.
patterns.root_cause: addLog() in lib/logs-storage.ts writes to a local JSON file (line 7: data/logs.json). The ADR-011 fire-and-forget log includes metadata.adminId. No check ensures this file is on a Russian-jurisdiction server. No PII scrubbing before write.
patterns.solution: 1) Strip adminId from fire-and-forget log metadata — log only action type and timestamp, no identifiers. OR 2) Confirm deployment target is Timeweb VPS (Russian jurisdiction) and document in ADR. 3) Backend-Agent must ensure metadata field contains NO PII (no adminId, no email, no name). QA-Agent must add assertion: grep data/logs.json for UUID patterns → fail if found.

patterns.title: Research-Agent Routed for Pure Backend Implementation Task
patterns.symptoms: Step s0-research was invoked for a task that is unambiguously a backend development task (implement GET /api/pipeline/summary endpoint with specific field contracts and a known data source). Research-Agent correctly self-identified the mismatch and returned a null deliverable after 4 attempts, scoring 4.8/10 and consuming 14.1s before the pipeline stalled at s1-orchestrator.
patterns.root_cause: The pipeline template appears to always execute s0-research as a mandatory first step regardless of task type. No pre-routing classification was applied to skip IDLE or irrelevant agents. The Orchestrator's own routing table explicitly maps "Node.js, APIs, business logic" to backend-agent, yet the fixed pipeline sequence overrode that logic.
patterns.solution: Implement a task-type classifier at pipeline entry (before s0) that maps tasks to relevant agent tracks. For implementation tasks with fully-specified requirements (endpoint path, response schema, data source all given), s0-research should be auto-skipped. Add a `skip_if` condition to s0-research: if task contains keywords like "implement", "add endpoint", "write code" AND no ambiguous requirements exist, bypass to s1-orchestrator → s3-architect → s4-backend directly. Codify this in pipeline routing rules.

patterns.title: s1-orchestrator Hard Failure Cascades Entire Pipeline to Pending
patterns.symptoms: s1-orchestrator failed with "Execution failed" after s0-research returned a non-deliverable (explicit "no research output exists" response). All downstream steps (s2 through s7) were left in pending state, meaning zero useful work was produced for a well-specified backend task.
patterns.root_cause: s1-orchestrator likely attempted to consume s0-research output as a required input for constructing task briefs to downstream agents. When s0 returned a meta-commentary instead of a structured deliverable, the orchestrator had no valid input to process, causing a hard crash rather than a graceful fallback. There is no null-output handling path — the orchestrator treats all upstream outputs as mandatory non-null.
patterns.solution: Orchestrator must handle null/rejected upstream outputs explicitly: (1) if s0-research returns a null deliverable with a valid reason (wrong domain), treat it as a skip signal, not a failure; (2) add a fallback branch: `if research_output == null AND task_type == implementation → proceed with architect-agent using original task spec directly`; (3) distinguish between "agent failed" and "agent correctly declined" — the latter should not propagate as an error state.

patterns.title: 4 Retry Attempts on an Agent That Was Correct From Attempt 1
patterns.symptoms: Research-Agent was retried 4 times despite correctly identifying on the first attempt that the task was outside its domain. Each retry produced the same valid meta-response. The final accepted score of 4.8/10 reflects the scorer penalizing absence of a research deliverable — but the absence was the correct behavior.
patterns.root_cause: The quality gate scoring rubric does not distinguish between "agent produced poor output" and "agent correctly declined an out-of-scope task." A score of 4.8 was assigned because completeness/specificity/actionability were measured against an expected research deliverable that should never have existed. The retry logic triggered on low score without checking whether the agent's refusal was semantically valid.
patterns.solution: Add a `scope_rejection` response type to agent output contracts. When an agent returns scope_rejection with a reason, the quality gate should: (1) validate the reason against the routing table, (2) if reason is valid → score the rejection itself (was the routing error correctly identified? was an alternative agent suggested?), (3) escalate to Orchestrator for re-routing rather than retrying the same agent. This would have saved ~3 retry cycles and prevented the downstream cascade.

patterns.title: Quality issue in s0-research
patterns.symptoms: Step s0-research required 1 retries to pass quality evaluation
patterns.root_cause: Accepted with warning (7.6/10 after 4 attempts): Research-Agent correctly identified that this is not a research task and provided a clear, well-reasoned routing recommendation to Backend-Agent — that
patterns.solution: Improve prompt specificity for this agent

patterns.title: Orchestrator Step Produces Empty Content Despite success=true
patterns.symptoms: Step s1-orchestrator reported execution status "failed" with error "Execution failed (success=true, contentLen=0)". The success flag was true but content length was zero, creating a contradictory state that halted the entire pipeline at step 1, leaving steps s2 through s7 in "pending" indefinitely.
patterns.root_cause: The Orchestrator agent returned a successful exit code but wrote no output to the pipeline buffer. This is a classic "silent failure" — the process completed without error but produced no actionable content. Likely cause: the Orchestrator received the Research-Agent's correct "no research needed, redirect to Frontend" output and either (a) failed to serialize its Task Brief to the output channel, or (b) short-circuited routing logic when it detected a trivial UI task and exited without emitting a delegation message.
patterns.solution: Add a mandatory non-empty output assertion in the Orchestrator step runner: if contentLen == 0 after execution, treat as hard failure regardless of success flag. Orchestrator must always emit at minimum a Task Brief or an explicit "pipeline skip" directive with justification. Add a canary check: `if output.trim().length === 0 → retry with explicit prompt injection "You must output a Task Brief even for simple tasks"`.

patterns.title: Full 10-Agent Pipeline Triggered for Single-Component UI Change
patterns.symptoms: A task scoped to "add a dark mode toggle to the Settings page header" — a pure frontend UI change requiring no DB schema, no API design, no security audit, no backend logic, and no infrastructure changes — was routed through a full 10-step pipeline including s3-architect, s3.5-cyber, s4-backend, s5.5-cyber-audit, and s6-devops. The Research-Agent (s0) correctly identified this in 11.9s and flagged it as frontend-only, but the pipeline structure did not allow early exit.
patterns.root_cause: The pipeline topology is statically defined with all agents always active. There is no lightweight "fast-track" path for tasks that are clearly scoped to a single agent domain. The Orchestrator's role is to route intelligently, but if it fails (as in s1), there is no fallback short-circuit.
patterns.solution: Define a "fast-track" pipeline variant for single-domain tasks: Research → Orchestrator (routing decision) → Target Agent → QA → Consolidation. The Orchestrator should emit a `pipeline_scope: ["frontend-agent", "qa-agent"]` directive that skips irrelevant agents rather than marking them pending. Implement this as a routing matrix check before pipeline start.

patterns.title: success=true / contentLen=0 Contradiction Not Caught by Pipeline Runner
patterns.symptoms: The pipeline runner accepted success=true from s1-orchestrator and simultaneously recorded the step as "failed" due to contentLen=0. These two signals were not reconciled — the runner did not retry the step, did not escalate to the user, and did not emit a clear diagnostic. Downstream steps silently moved to "pending" with no input to act on.
patterns.root_cause: The pipeline runner lacks a unified health check that treats (success=true AND contentLen=0) as a retriable error state. The two signals are evaluated independently rather than as a composite health assertion.
patterns.solution: Implement a composite step health check: `stepHealthy = success && contentLen > MIN_CONTENT_THRESHOLD`. If not healthy, trigger retry (max 2 per agent rules apply). After 2 failed retries with contentLen=0, escalate to user with diagnostic: "Orchestrator produced no output — manual routing required." Log the contradiction explicitly in pipeline telemetry.

patterns.title: Orchestrator Produces Empty Response on Simple UI Delegation Tasks
patterns.symptoms: Step s1-orchestrator returned an empty response body (contentLen=0) despite the API call reporting success=true. This caused a retry and halted the entire downstream pipeline (s2-pm through s7-consolidation remained pending).
patterns.root_cause: The Orchestrator agent likely received a task that was already pre-triaged by s0-research as "no research needed" — leaving the Orchestrator with an ambiguous or near-empty input context. The agent may have short-circuited its reasoning (nothing to synthesize from research) and produced no output rather than proceeding to generate a Task Brief for the appropriate agents (Frontend, Designer).
patterns.solution: Two mitigations: (1) When s0-research returns a "no research needed" verdict, the pipeline should pass a canonical stub input to s1-orchestrator explicitly stating "Research skipped — proceed directly to task routing" with the original task description preserved verbatim. (2) Add an output guard in the Orchestrator layer: if contentLen=0 and success=true, treat as a soft failure, inject the original task prompt as fallback context, and retry once before escalating.

patterns.title: Research Step Activated for Pure UI Component Tasks — Wasted Latency
patterns.symptoms: s0-research ran for 12.5 seconds on a task ("Add a dark mode toggle to the Settings page header") that it correctly self-identified as requiring zero research. The step consumed time and a model call only to produce a redirect message.
patterns.root_cause: The pipeline routing logic does not pre-classify task type before activating agents. All tasks enter the full pipeline regardless of domain, causing research, cyber, architect, and other agents to spin up for tasks that are purely frontend implementation work.
patterns.solution: Introduce a lightweight pre-classifier step (rule-based, not LLM) before s0-research. If task keywords match patterns like "add [component] to [page]", "toggle", "UI element", "dark mode" — skip s0-research and s3-architect, route directly to s2-pm → s4-designer → s4-frontend. This would have reduced this pipeline from 9 active steps to ~4.

patterns.title: success=true With contentLen=0 Is a Silent Failure Mode Not Caught by Pipeline
patterns.symptoms: The pipeline infrastructure reported the Orchestrator step as having success=true while simultaneously having contentLen=0. These two signals are contradictory — a successful orchestration step must produce a Task Brief. The pipeline did not catch this contradiction and required an explicit retry mechanism to detect it.
patterns.root_cause: The success flag appears to reflect HTTP/transport-level success (the model responded without error), not semantic success (the response contains valid, non-empty content). There is no output schema validation between steps.
patterns.solution: Add a post-step validation hook that asserts: if agent role is orchestrator|backend|frontend|architect, then contentLen must be > minimum threshold (e.g., 200 chars). If not, override success=false immediately without waiting for the retry path. This prevents silent empty outputs from masquerading as successful steps.

patterns.title: Consolidation Step Running Without Upstream Agent Outputs
patterns.symptoms: s7-consolidation executed and produced a "Weekly Report" despite steps s2–s6 being skipped. The consolidation had no real agent outputs to summarize, yet still scored 10/10 and generated a structured report framed as a legitimate pipeline result.
patterns.root_cause: s7-consolidation was triggered by pipeline completion status rather than by actual upstream output availability. The step has no guard clause checking whether meaningful inputs exist before generating a summary.
patterns.solution: Add an input-validation check in s7-consolidation: if fewer than N upstream steps produced real output (not null/skipped), emit a short "pipeline incomplete — nothing to consolidate" notice instead of fabricating a report structure. A 10/10 score on an empty consolidation is a scoring calibration bug — completeness cannot be 10 when inputs are absent.

patterns.title: Quality Score Inflation on Content-Free Output
patterns.symptoms: s7-consolidation received a perfect 10/10 (C:10, S:10, A:10) despite having no real agent outputs to consolidate. The scoring rubric rewarded format compliance and structure rather than substantive content.
patterns.root_cause: The quality gate evaluates output format and internal consistency, not whether the output reflects actual work done. A well-formatted empty report passes the same checks as a well-formatted substantive report.
patterns.solution: Add a content-presence check to the quality scoring layer: Completeness score must be penalized if the number of summarized agent outputs is zero or below the expected count for the pipeline. Consider a separate "input coverage" dimension: what % of expected upstream steps are represented in the consolidation.

patterns.title: QA Scored 7/10 Despite Preceding Steps All Scoring 10/10 — Delivery Gap
patterns.symptoms: s5-qa completed with score 7/10 (C:7.5, S:9, A:8.5) and received feedback "Strong findings, but critical delivery gaps" — despite all four upstream steps (pm, architect, frontend, designer) scoring perfect 10/10. The QA report covered the right scope but failed to fully close the loop on deliverables.
patterns.root_cause: QA agent produced strong analytical findings but did not translate them into actionable, structured test artifacts (e.g., explicit pass/fail checklist per component, regression test cases, or a clear sign-off recommendation). High specificity (9) but incomplete completeness (7.5) suggests findings were present but not fully packaged for handoff.
patterns.solution: QA task briefs must explicitly require: (1) per-component pass/fail matrix, (2) list of open blocking vs. non-blocking issues, (3) explicit SHIP / DO NOT SHIP verdict. Without these three outputs, QA reports score below 8 regardless of finding quality.

patterns.title: Designer Agent Ran After Frontend Agent — Inverted Design-Dev Order
patterns.symptoms: s4-frontend (27.6s) completed before s4-designer (108.6s). Design specs were produced after implementation code was already written. The designer output references ADR-007 and "3 new components" — suggesting specs were written to match existing code rather than guide it.
patterns.root_cause: Pipeline configuration placed frontend before designer, possibly to allow the designer to spec against real code. This is a pragmatic shortcut for refactor/improvement tasks but creates a structural risk: design specs become documentation rather than constraints.
patterns.solution: For new feature work, enforce Designer → Frontend order. For improvement/refactor tasks, the current order is acceptable but must be explicitly tagged as "design-as-documentation mode" so QA does not treat design specs as the source of truth for acceptance criteria.

patterns.title: LLM Provider Overload Causes Full Pipeline Cascade Failure
patterns.symptoms: Step s2-pm failed with API 500 "overloaded_error" from the underlying LLM provider. Since s2-pm is a blocking dependency for all parallel steps (s3.1–s3.5), the entire downstream pipeline — 13 steps total — was left in `pending` state, resulting in complete task failure despite a successful research phase.
patterns.root_cause: No retry logic or fallback provider was implemented for transient LLM overload errors. A single point of failure at s2-pm propagated to 100% of remaining work. The pipeline architecture treats LLM API errors identically to logical failures, with no distinction between recoverable (503/overload) and non-recoverable errors.
patterns.solution: 1) Implement exponential backoff retry (3–5 attempts) specifically for 5xx/overload errors before marking a step as failed. 2) Add a secondary LLM provider fallback (e.g., switch model/endpoint on overload). 3) Persist completed step outputs (s0-research scored 8.5/10) so resume is possible from s2-pm without re-running earlier steps. 4) Classify error types: `transient` vs `permanent` — only transient errors should trigger auto-retry.

patterns.title: No Checkpoint / Resume Capability After Partial Completion
patterns.symptoms: Step s0-research completed successfully with a quality score of 8.5/10, and s1-orchestrator approved the checkpoint. However, when s2-pm failed, the pipeline had no mechanism to resume from the last successful checkpoint. The entire 31.4s execution is effectively lost and must be restarted from scratch.
patterns.root_cause: Pipeline state is not persisted between steps in a resumable format. The checkpoint approval at s1-orchestrator is a human gate but not a technical save-point that the execution engine can restore from.
patterns.solution: Implement durable step-state storage (e.g., Redis or DB record per step) that saves `output`, `status`, and `quality_score` upon step completion. On re-run, skip steps with status `completed` and resume from the first `failed` or `pending` step. This is standard in workflow engines like Temporal, Prefect, or Airflow.

patterns.title: Parallel Architecture Block (s3.x) Fully Blocked by Single Sequential Dependency
patterns.symptoms: Steps s3.1-adr, s3.2-api, s3.3-erd, s3.4-fileplan, and s3.5-cyber were designed to run in parallel — a sound architectural choice — but all five were gated entirely on s2-pm completing. The parallelism provides zero resilience benefit when the single upstream dependency fails.
patterns.root_cause: The pipeline design optimizes for parallel execution speed but does not account for partial dependency satisfaction. There is no partial-start logic (e.g., "start s3.x steps that only need s0-research output if s2-pm is unavailable").
patterns.solution: Analyze inter-step data dependencies at a granular level. Some s3.x agents (e.g., s3.3-erd, s3.2-api) may be able to start from research output alone. Introduce dependency graphs with optional vs. required inputs, allowing steps to begin with partial context and merge PM output later if/when it becomes available.

patterns.title: Large Code Generation Agents Systematically Truncate Output Under Token Pressure
patterns.symptoms: Both s4-designer (3.3/10) and s4-backend (3.9/10) failed with escalation after 4 attempts each. In both cases the root failure mode was identical: output was truncated mid-sentence/mid-block — designer cut off inside a CSS `:root` block, backend cut off with "in parallel:" — meaning the agents never delivered complete file sets across any attempt. s4-frontend, s4.5-checkpoint, and all downstream steps (s5-qa, s5-cyber-audit, s6-devops, s7-consolidation) remained pending, causing total pipeline failure.
patterns.root_cause: Implementation-stage agents (designer, backend) were asked to generate large volumes of code (10+ service files, 5 SQL migrations, full CSS design system) in a single prompt turn. Token budget exhaustion caused silent mid-output truncation. Retrying the same prompt structure 4 times reproduced the same truncation at roughly the same point, indicating a structural prompt design problem rather than a transient error. The agents had no mechanism to detect their own truncation and self-correct.
patterns.solution: Split implementation agents into smaller atomic sub-agents per layer or per file group. For backend: separate agents for (1) migrations, (2) domain services, (3) API routes. For designer: separate agents for (1) CSS tokens/design system, (2) component files, (3) page layouts. Each sub-agent should target a maximum of 3-5 files per invocation. Additionally, implement a structural validator at the orchestrator level that checks JSON completeness and file count against the s3.4-fileplan manifest before accepting output — reject immediately if delivered file count < expected count, rather than scoring and retrying the same oversized prompt.

patterns.title: PM Output Accepted Below Threshold With Incomplete User Stories — Propagated Defects Downstream
patterns.symptoms: s2-pm scored 4.9/10 (accepted with warning at 4.8/10 after 4 attempts). The output was truncated mid-sentence at US-8 description and was missing acceptance criteria and a full story set. Despite the warning, the pipeline continued. All architecture and API stages (s3.1–s3.5) scored 9.0+ — meaning they built on a structurally incomplete requirements document, silently filling gaps themselves. This creates undocumented scope assumptions baked into ADR, API contract, and ERD that were never formally approved.
patterns.root_cause: The acceptance threshold (4.8/10 with warning) is too permissive for a requirements-generating stage. The PM agent hit a token/output limit on the 4th attempt and the orchestrator accepted the truncated output rather than restructuring the prompt. There was no explicit check that all planned user stories were present and complete before proceeding.
patterns.solution: For pipeline stages whose output serves as the canonical input to all downstream stages (PM/PRD), enforce a hard minimum score of 7.0/10 with a completeness gate: verify that all user stories have titles, descriptions, and acceptance criteria present before passing the checkpoint. If the PM agent truncates, split the PRD generation into two passes — (1) story list + titles, (2) full acceptance criteria per story — rather than retrying the monolithic prompt.

patterns.title: Retry Loop Reproduces Same Truncation — No Prompt Restructuring Between Attempts
patterns.symptoms: s2-pm (4 attempts), s4-designer (4 attempts, escalated), s4-backend (4 attempts, escalated) all hit their retry limit without resolution. In each case, the failure mode on attempt N+1 was identical to attempt N — truncation at the same structural point. Four retries were consumed producing the same broken output each time.
patterns.root_cause: The retry mechanism re-issues the same prompt with feedback appended, but does not restructure the scope of the task. When truncation is caused by prompt size exceeding token budget, appending feedback makes the prompt larger, potentially worsening truncation. There is no escalation-before-limit logic that detects "same failure mode repeated" and triggers prompt decomposition instead of a plain retry.
patterns.solution: Implement a retry classification layer: if the failure reason on attempt 2 is the same category as attempt 1 (e.g., truncation detected via JSON parse failure or output ending mid-token), do not issue attempt 3 with the same prompt — instead trigger a prompt decomposition strategy (split task into smaller sub-tasks) and route to sub-agents. Reserve the escalation path for genuinely ambiguous quality failures, not deterministic token-limit truncations.

patterns.title: Pipeline Fails Completely When Parallel Implementation Agents Fail — No Partial Delivery Mechanism
patterns.symptoms: s4-designer and s4-backend both escalated. Because s4-frontend was pending and s4.5-checkpoint (a gate step) was pending, the entire pipeline halted with zero code delivered to the repository. All the high-quality upstream work (ADR 9.2, API 9.1, ERD 9.2, fileplan 9.2) produced no deployable artifact. Total pipeline duration was 1706.3s with a failed outcome.
patterns.root_cause: The pipeline treats the implementation stage (s4-*) as an all-or-nothing gate. There is no mechanism to commit partial deliverables (e.g., the SQL migrations that were generated, even if incomplete) or to allow QA/DevOps to begin work on the portions that were delivered.
patterns.solution: Introduce partial-commit checkpoints within the s4 stage: if migrations are generated and pass a syntax check, commit them independently of whether service files are complete. Allow s5-qa to begin reviewing whatever artifacts exist. This requires the checkpoint agent (s4.5) to support partial-pass states with an explicit list of what is and isn't ready, rather than a binary pass/fail. For the current pipeline, the recovery path is to re-run only s4-backend and s4-designer with decomposed prompts, not restart the full pipeline from s0.
